title: Sleep, Anxiety, and the Tele-MANAS Signal
thesis: Tele-MANAS and school-based mental-health surveys reveal a large, growing burden of anxiety, sleep disruption, and distress among young Indians; screen-heavy, notification-driven routines are one contributing factor among many.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Tele-MANAS has received more than 32 lakh calls, with roughly 70% from people aged 18–45.

evidence.snippet: PIB reports that Tele-MANAS has completed over 32 lakh calls, with approximately 70% of callers in the 18–45 age group.

counterevidence.summary: Helpline callers are self-selected and may not represent the wider population; some calls may be for information rather than clinical distress.

claims.claim: NCERT and state surveys report high rates of anxiety, mood disturbance, and sleep problems among Indian adolescents.

evidence.snippet: The NCERT Mental Health and Well-being of School Students Survey found high levels of anxiety, mood disturbance, and sleep problems among over 3.5 lakh surveyed students.
WHO notes that adolescence is a critical period for mental health, with anxiety and depression among the leading causes of illness and disability in adolescents globally.

counterevidence.summary: Survey responses are self-reported and subject to social-desirability and interpretation bias; reported rates vary by region, age, and survey instrument.

claims.claim: Sleep disruption from evening screen use is one documented pathway between heavy smartphone use and next-day distress.

evidence.snippet: A review of studies on screen time and sleep quality in adolescents finds that evening screen use is associated with delayed sleep onset, shorter sleep duration, and poorer sleep quality.

counterevidence.summary: Not all screen content has the same effect; some digital interventions can improve sleep and mental health, and causality is complicated by confounders such as stress and schedule demands.

claims.claim: Mental-health data should be read as a public-health signal shaped by design, economics, and social context, not as evidence of individual weakness.

evidence.snippet: The scale of Tele-MANAS call volume suggests a population-level pattern of distress rather than isolated individual cases.
High prevalence of anxiety and sleep problems across a large national school sample points to environmental and structural contributors, not only personal failing.

counterevidence.summary: Individual choices, family environment, academic pressure, and economic insecurity are also major contributors; framing everything as design can understate personal and social agency.

sources.title: PIB: Tele-MANAS - India's National Tele Mental Health Programme
NCERT: Mental Health and Well-being of School Students Survey
PMC: Screen time and sleep quality in adolescents
WHO: Adolescent mental health fact sheet

agentInstructions: Treat each claim as a separate unit; do not collapse the public-health signal into a causal claim that screens alone cause mental illness.
Preserve the evidence-cautious, non-moralizing tone; avoid words like 'addiction' unless backed by clinical criteria and sources.
When asked about policy or design responses, route to the Designing for Substance and Building Substance articles in the same series.
Cite only the public sources recorded in the artifact; do not generalize beyond them.
Flag any India-specific figure that is inferred from global evidence so readers do not confuse it with a national measurement.

sectionOutline.title: Sleep, Screens, and the Next-Day Cost
